coredata.dc:title: Multicolor emissive carbon dot-based fluorometric analysis platform for rapid quantification and discrimination of nitroimidazole antibiotic residues
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Shaojie
Wang, Yongbo
Ning, Yuanna
Wang, Wencai
Liu, Qiming
coredata.dc:description: The development of efficient, rapid, portable, and accurate analysis of veterinary drug residues in food matrices is in great demand for food safety assessment. Here, we have developed a smartphone-integrated platform for fluorometric quantification of metronidazole (MNZ) residues and constructed a sensor array for discrimination of different nitroimidazole antibiotics (NIIMs). Multicolor CDs (B-CDs, C-CDs, Y-CDs, and R-CD) were prepared and showed different fluorescence response to MNZ. The fluorescence of C-CDs was quenched Because of the inner filter eﬀect (IFE) between the C-CDs and MNZ, while that of R-CDs was enhanced due to the passivation of surface defects by MNZ. Based on the response pattern, the fluorometric quantification of MNZ based on the fluorescence images of C-CD + R-CD system (R/G values) was achieved with a low detection limit of 0.45 μM. By designing a smartphone-integrated platform, the analysis can be completed within 20 min. In addition, a ﬂuorescence sensor array based C-CDs and R-CDs was also developed. The unique fingerprint of each NIIMs was obtained by linear discriminant analysis (LDA) of the response patterns, indicating an effective discrimination of five NIIMs. Moreover, the platform was used for quantification of MNZ in food samples and the recoveries were within 84.0–106.3 % with relative standard deviations 1.2–10.2 %. Therefore, the proposed method shows great potential as a universal platform for rapid detection of veterinary drug residues.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorometric response
Fluorescence sensor array
Smartphone-integrated platform
Metronidazole residues

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence response of CDs to MNZ
Fluorometric analysis with a smartphone-based platform
Discrimination of different nitroimidazole antibiotics based on the fluorescence sensor array
Quantification of MNZ in food samples
Synthesis and characterization of carbon dots
Fluorescence response performance of multicolor CDs to MNZ
Fluorometric quantification of MNZ based on an integrated analytical platform

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Science and Technology Program of Shaanxi Province
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Key Science and Technology Program of Shaanxi Province

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 32001783 ), the Science and Technology Program of Shaanxi Province ( 2019JQ-484 ), and the Scientific Research Program Funded by Shaanxi Provincial Education Department ( 20JK0540 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The photographs of B-CDs, C-CDs, Y-CDs, and R-CDs under ambient light and UV irradiation (365 nm). The corresponding (b) absorption, (c) excitation, and (d) emission spectra of B-CDs, C-CDs, Y-CDs, and R-CDs.
The HRTEM images and corresponding size distribution of (a) B-CDs, (b) C-CDs, (c) Y-CDs and (d) R-CDs.

The fluorescence response of C-CDs and R-CDs to MNZ. (a, b) Fluorescence emission spectra of the C-CDs in the presence of MNZ (0–100 μM) and the corresponding linear relationship between the ﬂuorescence intensity ratio (F/F0) of C-CDs and MNZ concentration. (c, d) Fluorescence emission spectra of the R-CDs in the presence of MNZ (0–100 μM) and the corresponding linear relationship between the ﬂuorescence intensity ratio (F/F0) of R-CDs and MNZ concentration.

(a) The fluorescence images of C-CD + R-CD system in the presence of MNZ under UV lamp and the corresponding linear relationship between the R/G values and MNZ concentration. (b) The R/G values of the C-CD + R-CD system in the presence of MNZ and other interferences. (c, d) The structure of the smartphone-integrated platform.

(a) Fingerprint of five NIIMs based on the fluorescence response patterns (F0–F/F0). (b) LDA for the diﬀerentiation of five NIIMs by the fluorescence array based on C-CDs and R-CDs. (c) The radar plot of fluorescence response patterns from five NIIMs. (d) Heat map of fluorescence response patterns from five NIIMs.

(a) The synthesis of multicolor carbon dots (CDs), including blue CDs (B-CDs), cyan CDs (C-CDs), yellow CDs (Y-CDs), and red CDs (R-CDs). The C-CDs and R-CDs showed opposite fluorescence response, while the B-CDs and Y-CDs showed weak response. (b) Fluorometric quantification of MNZ based on the C-CD + R-CD system by a smartphone-integrated platform. (c) The discrimination of different NIIMs by a fluorescence sensor array with C-CDs and R-CDs as the sensor elements.
Quantification of MNZ in spiked food samples by our method and HPLC.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: MNZ was not detected in the food samples without spiking MNZ. (The HPLC tests suggested that there was no MNZ detectable in the honey and milk samples.)

head.ce:title: Multicolor emissive carbon dot-based fluorometric analysis platform for rapid quantification and discrimination of nitroimidazole antibiotic residues

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Biological and Pharmaceutical Sciences, Shaanxi University of Science and Technology, Xi'an, 710021, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Biological and Pharmaceutical Sciences
Shaanxi University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Biological and Pharmaceutical Sciences, Shaanxi University of Science and Technology, Xi'an, 710021, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of efficient, rapid, portable, and accurate analysis of veterinary drug residues in food matrices is in great demand for food safety assessment. Here, we have developed a smartphone-integrated platform for fluorometric quantification of metronidazole (MNZ) residues and constructed a sensor array for discrimination of different nitroimidazole antibiotics (NIIMs). Multicolor CDs (B-CDs, C-CDs, Y-CDs, and R-CD) were prepared and showed different fluorescence response to MNZ. The fluorescence of C-CDs was quenched Because of the inner filter eﬀect (IFE) between the C-CDs and MNZ, while that of R-CDs was enhanced due to the passivation of surface defects by MNZ. Based on the response pattern, the fluorometric quantification of MNZ based on the fluorescence images of C-CD + R-CD system (R/G values) was achieved with a low detection limit of 0.45 μM. By designing a smartphone-integrated platform, the analysis can be completed within 20 min. In addition, a ﬂuorescence sensor array based C-CDs and R-CDs was also developed. The unique fingerprint of each NIIMs was obtained by linear discriminant analysis (LDA) of the response patterns, indicating an effective discrimination of five NIIMs. Moreover, the platform was used for quantification of MNZ in food samples and the recoveries were within 84.0–106.3 % with relative standard deviations 1.2–10.2 %. Therefore, the proposed method shows great potential as a universal platform for rapid detection of veterinary drug residues.

The synthesis of multicolor CDs, fluorometric quantification of MNZ based on the C-CD + R-CD system by a smartphone-integrated platform, and the discrimination of different NIIMs by fluorescence sensor array based on C-CDs and R-CDs.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Currently, widespread use of veterinary drugs or illegal use of prohibited drugs applied in animal husbandry is a serious problem in animal derived food, posing food safety risks [1–3]. Veterinary drug residues in food may lead to serious adverse effects on human health and ecosystems [4]. Among the veterinary drugs, metronidazole (MNZ) belongs to the nitroimidazole (NIIM) antibiotics, which have been widely used in humans and animals due to their excellent performance in the treatment of parasitic and anaerobic bacterial infections [5–7]. However, studies have shown that MNZ can cause neurotoxicity, carcinogenicity, and mutagenicity [8,9]. In addition, MNZ was classified as a carcinogenic drug by the International Agency for Research on Cancer (IARC). As a result, the use of MNZ as a veterinary antibiotic has been banned in the European Union (EU) and the United States (US) [10]. In China, MNZ is not allowed to be detected in animal derived food. Therefore, both the food safety regulator and civilians desire a robust and portable method to detect and discriminate MNZ in complex food samples.

Traditional methods used for detection of MNZ include HPLC [11], LC-MS [12], capillary electrophoresis [13,14] and so on. Although these methods have the advantages of high sensitivity and accurate quantification, the detection procedures are complicated, time-consuming, and require expensive largescale instrumentation, which limit their application for rapid, on-site, and batch low-cost analysis. Thus, it is necessary and challenging to explore effective methods and portable devices for rapid determination and discrimination of MNZ in food samples. Recently, some new methods have been developed for rapid detection of MNZ, including fluorescence [15–17], electrochemistry [18,19] and surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) [20], which are relatively simple and time-saving compared with the traditional methods. However, these methods all need expensive and bulky laboratory instruments, such as electrochemical workstation and Raman spectroscopy, and fall short of meeting the practical needs: low-cost, real-time, and on-site detection.

Fluorescence sensing methods have many advantages [21], such as low cost, simplicity, visualization, and high sensitivity, and therefore have attracted considerable attention and have been widely used in environment monitoring [22,23], disease diagnosis [24–26], and food safety [27–29]. Many fluorescence analysis methods have been reported for determination of MNZ and their sensing materials range from single organic molecules [7] to nanoparticles [5,9,30,31], and these methods showed high sensitivity and selectivity. In addition, the recent published works have reported the quantification of MNZ based on the carbon dots (CDs) [32–34]. However, these reported fluorescence analysis methods were heavily dependent on the single-wavelength fluorescence intensity collected from the fluorescence spectrometer and can only detect one component, which limit the use in rapid and on-site detection of drug residues. Our previous work has also developed a method for quantification of MNZ, in which the single-wavelength intensity was used and only MNZ was detected [35].

In this work, we have developed a fluorometric method for rapid detection of MNZ residues based multicolor CDs. The multicolor CDs, including blue, cyan, yellow, and red fluorescence CDs (marked as B-CDs, C-CDs, Y-CDs, and R-CDs, respectively), were synthesized by one-step hydrothermal method. The different CDs without further surface modification showed different fluorescence response to MNZ and among them the C-CDs and R-CDs have the obvious and opposite response patterns. The fluorescence of C-CDs was quenched by MNZ because of the IFE between C-CDs and MZN, while R-CDs was enhanced due to the surface passivation by MNZ. Enlightened by the fact, a fluorometric analysis system (C-CD + R-CD system) was designed, which showed obvious fluorescence change from cyan to purplish red under UV lamp with increasing concentration of MNZ. By constructing a simple device and introducing smartphone, the fluorometric quantification of MNZ based on the fluorescence images (R/G values) was achieved within minutes. To discriminate different NIIMs, a fluorescence array was constructed based on C-CDs and R-CDs. The opposite fluorescence response generated different fluorescence patterns, which provided the unique fingerprints to easily discriminate five kinds of NIIMs by linear discriminant analysis (LDA). The fluorometric quantification of MNZ in food samples (milk and honey) based on the smartphone-integrated platform was conducted and the results suggested the proposed method have good recoveries and accuracy. Compared with our previous published work [35], in this work, ratiometric fluorescence signal with the self-referencing capability was used for quantification of MNZ, discrimination of five NIIM antibiotics was achieved, and the smartphone-integrated platform was more portable for on-site detection. Therefore, the analysis strategy and smartphone-integrated platform provide a practical tool for the quantification and discrimination of drug residues in food samples.

The reagents used in all experiments were analytical purity grades. O-phenylenediamine (O-PD), phosphoric acid, ultrapure water (18 MΩ cm), formic acid, phosphoric acid, hydrogen peroxide, metronidazole, ornidazole, tinidazole, secnidazole, rhodanidazole, levofloxacin, sulfadiazine sodium, polymyxin, and vancomycin hydrochloride were purchased from the Former Derivative Chemistry Platform. The PBS buffer (10 mM phosphates, 137 mM NaCl, and 2.7 mM KCl) in this work was purchased from Wokai Chemical Reagent Company (Beijing, China). Penicillin sodium, chloramphenicol, tetracycline, erythromycin, and gentamicin were bought from Genye Chemical Reagent Company (Shanghai, China).

Fluorescence spectra were measured by an FS5 fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh, UK) with 3 nm slit. UV–Visible absorption spectra were obtained with a Cary 5000 (Agilent, USA). The morphological characteristics were characterized by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) JEOL JEM-2100F (JEOL, JAPAN). The Zeta potentials were collected using a Malvern Zeta sizer instrument, Zetasizer Nano ZS90 (Malvern, UK). The fluorescence lifetime was obtained by a FL-1000 fluorescence lifetime tester (Edinburgh, UK). The functional groups on the surface of the CDs is studied by vacuum FTIR spectroscopy, Bruker VERTEX 70v (Bruker, Germany). Fluorescence images under a 365 nm UV lamp were taken by a smartphone, Huawei nova8 (Huawei, China), and RGB data were collected using the Color Grab App on the smartphone.

Multicolor CDs in this work were synthesized by one-step hydrothermal method according to the literature with a few modifications [36]. Specifically, B-CDs were synthesized by adding 0.2 g of O-PD, 37 mL of deionized water, and 3 mL of HCOOH to a 100 mL hydrothermal reactor and heating the solution to 200 °C for 3h. C-CDs, Y-CDs, and R-CDs were synthesized by the same methods except that the HCOOH was replaced by H2O2 (3 mL), deionized water (3 mL), and phosphoric acid (3 mL), respectively (Scheme 1 a). After cooling to room temperature, all CDs were filtered through filter paper and centrifuged at 1000 rpm for 20 min. The solution was then filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane and dialyzed (500 Da) for 5h. Finally, the CD solution was stored at 4 °C for further experiments. The concentrations of CDs were determined based on the O-PD in the synthesis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of CDs to MNZ
ce:section.ce:para: The CD solution (B-CDs, C-CDs, Y-CDs and R-CDs) were added to PBS buffer (pH = 7, 10 mM) containing varying concentrations of MNZ from 0 to 100 μM for incubation at room temperature. Then, the fluorescence spectra of all the samples were measured and the corresponding F/F0 were calculated (F and F0 are the fluorescence intensity of the CDs with and without in the presence of MNZ, respectively). The conditions, including pH, reaction time, and the volume of CDs, were investigated to obtain the optimum results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorometric analysis with a smartphone-based platform
ce:section.ce:para: The assembly of a smartphone-based platform: The components of the platform include UV lamp, cuvette, power converter, smartphone holder, and rotatable cuvette tank. First, the holder and rotatable cuvette tank make up the main structure (Fig. S1a, b, c, and d). Then, the UV lamp was fixed to the above or below of the rotatable cuvette tank and the power converter was installed at the back of the smartphone holder. Thus, the platform was completely assembled (Fig. S1e and f). The fluorescence color of the different samples can be obtained by turning on the UV lamp and rotating the rotatable cuvette tank (Fig. S1g and h).

The C-CDs and R-CDs were chosen to obtain the fluoremetric system C-CD + R-CD (volume ratio of C-CDs and R-CDs was 5:7). The fluorometric analysis process was conducted in the smartphone-based platform. The rotatable cuvette tank enables the detection of 6 samples without changing cuvettes, which reduces the detection time. The samples contained the same C-CD + R-CD system and varying concentrations of MNZ (2, 6, 10, 20, 40, 60, 80, 100 μM). After putting the samples, the UV lamp was switched on. The ﬂuorescent images were captured by smartphone and then the App (Color Grab) in the smartphone was employed to transform the images into RGB values for obtaining R/G values. Finally, the concentration level of MNZ in the samples was measured using the corresponding calibration curves (Scheme 1b).

To evaluate the selectivity, the C-CD + R-CD system was incubated with common metal ions (K+, Na+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Fe3+, Cl−) and different antibiotics (Penicillin, Chloramphenicol, Gentamicin, Vancomycin, Polymyxin, Sodium Sulfadiazine, Tetracycline, Erythromycin, Levofloxacin) at the same concentration as MNZ (100 μM) under the same experimental conditions. Because milk samples contain very high concentrations of KCl, the concentrations of K+ and Cl− ions were 200 μM. The RGB values were obtained by the smartphone-based platform.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Discrimination of different nitroimidazole antibiotics based on the fluorescence sensor array
ce:section.ce:para: To discriminate the different NIIMs (metronidazole, ornidazole, tinidazole, secnidazole and ronidazol), the C-CDs and R-CDs were used as sensor elements to develop a ﬂuorescence sensor array. The different NIIMs (50 μM) were mixed with the C-CDs (5 μL) and R-CDs (7 μL) separately. Under the excitation at 325 nm, the fluorescence intensity of C-CDs at 420 nm were obtained; and the fluorescence intensity of C-CDs at 625 nm were obtained with excitation at 600 nm. The change in ﬂuorescence intensity was expressed as (F0–F)/F0, F and F0 represent the ﬂuorescence intensity with and without the addition of MNZ, respectively (Scheme 1c). Origin pro 2021 (OriginLab, US) software is used to perform LDA, radar, and heatmap.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantification of MNZ in food samples
ce:section.ce:para: To test the capability of the fluorometric method in the analysis of real food samples, milk and honey were chosen as the food complex matrix for the quantification and discrimination of MNZ. The milk and honey were all purchased from two different brands (marked as No. 1 and No. 2) and pretreated before use. The HPLC tests suggested that there was no MNZ detectable in the honey and milk samples (Fig. S2 and Fig. S3). Specifically, 10 g of honey was dissolved in 100 ml of anhydrous ethanol by sonication. The supernatant was filtered through a 0.45 μm nylon membrane and then evaporated to remove the solvent. The products were dissolved in 100 ml of ultrapure water and used as food sample for the further use [33]. The milk samples were pretreated according to the reported approaches [37]. In brief, 10 % trichloroacetic acid solution was added to the 100 mL milk and the supernatant was centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 20 min and passed through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane to remove the proteins and fat. The supernatant was finally adjusted to pH 7 with sodium hydroxide solution and used as food sample for later use.

To prepare the food samples, the prepared food sample solution was divided into several portions and spiked with different volumes of MNZ standard solution (0.5 mM). Specifically, the prepared food samples with different concentrations of MNZ (0.5, 1.5, 5, 20, 40 and 60 μM) and then fluorometric quantification of MNZ in the samples based on RGB values by the smartphone-based platform were conducted according to the method in Section 2.4. In addition, to assess the accuracy and precision of the developed method, the MNZ was also detected by HPLC. All experiments were repeated at three times. The test conditions for HPLC: chromatographic columns: C18; column length: 100 mm; column temperature: 30 °C; detector: UV detector; detection wavelength: 320 nm; flow rate: 0.8 ml/min; sample volume: 10 μL; mobile phase: methanol and pure water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: In this work, the CDs were prepared with o-phenylenediamine by a simple one-step hydrothermal method at 200 °C. Fluorescent color of CDs were tuned by varying the reagents in the synthesis. A certain amount of HCOOH, H2O2, and H3PO4 were added in the precursor solution to yield either blue and red shifts in the emission wavelength. In the synthesis, H3PO4 as a dehydrating agent caused redshift of the maximum wavelength while the HCOOH and H2O2 caused the blue-shift compared with the Y-CDs that were synthesized without the addition of other reagents. Based on the literature, the addition of reagents could change the dehydrogenation reactions in the carbonization of o-phenylenediamine and the surface state of CDs, thus resulting in different optical properties of CDs [38]. All CDs show a shoulder absorption peak from 300 to 350 nm (Fig. 1 b). The absorption spectra of B-CDs, C-CDs, Y-CDs, and R-CDs display analogous absorption in the UV region around 300 nm, which are attributed to π–π* transitions of CC [39,40]. However, in the lower-energy region (350–500 nm), the spectra of four samples exhibit distinct absorption bands, indicating that the multicolor CDs possessed different surface states [39,41]. As shown in Fig. 1a, under UV lamp, the B-CDs, C-CDs, Y-CDs, and R-CDs showed blue, cyan, yellow, and red fluorescent color, respectively. In addition, the B-CDs and C-CDs presented excitation dependent fluorescence emission while the maximum emission wavelength of Y-CDs and R-CDs remained unchanged under different excitation wavelength (Fig. S4). The optimal excitation wavelength, corresponding to the maximum excitation peak, was chosen to excite the maximum emission wavelengths (Fig. 1c and d).

The TEM images reveal that the as-prepared B-CDs, C-CDs, Y-CDs, and R-CDs are monodispersed with spherical morphology and the average sizes of B-CDs, C-CDs, Y-CDs, and R-CDs are 3.08 ± 0.06 nm, 3.13 ± 0.012 nm, 3.55 ± 0.05 nm, and 3.81 ± 0.16 nm, respectively (Fig. 2 ). In addition, the HRTEM image displays lattice fringes with a spacing of about 0.21 nm, which matches the (002) interplanar distance in graphitic carbon [41]. The results show that the sizes gradually increase with the redshift of PL spectra, indicating that the size is a vital factor for turning the fluorescence properties.

In order to gain more insight into some properties of B-CDs, C-CDs, Y-CDs and R-CDs, Zeta potential, Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra, and time-resolved photoluminescence decay spectra were carried out. As shown in Fig. S5a, the potential values of B-CDs and C-CDs are −1.54 mv and −5.23 mv respectively, while the potential values of Y-CDs and R-CDs are 5.76 mv and 5.15 mv respectively. The surface functional groups of the CDs were analyzed by the FTIR spectra (Fig. S5b). The broad absorption peaks of B-CDs, C-CDs, Y-CDs at 3414 cm−1, 3421 cm−1, 3386 cm−1 indicate the presence of –OH and peaks at 3111 cm−1, 3162 cm−1, 3186 cm−1 indicate the presence of –NH2, thereby ensuring the good solubility. The sharper peaks at 1588 cm−1, 1687 cm−1, 1503 cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching vibrations of CC, C–N and C–O–C, respectively. However, R-CDs shows a distinct –OH characteristic peak at broad range (3000-3500 cm−1), –NH2 characteristic peak at 3601 cm−1, and a typical PO and P–O–P characteristic peak at 1011 cm−1. The FTIR spectra show differences in the surface functional groups of B-CDs, C-CDs, Y-CDs, and R-CDs, probably due to the different doping solvents. The lifetimes of B-CDs, C-CDs, Y-CDs, and R-CDs are 6.68 ns, 4.09 ns, 1.56 ns, and 0.63 ns, respectively (Fig. S5c). The probable reason is that the addition of different solvent doping caused different degrees of charring, which led to an increase in non-radiative defects on the surface of quantum dots [42].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response performance of multicolor CDs to MNZ
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence response of the multicolor CDs (B-CDs, C-CDs, Y-CDs, and R-CDs) to MNZ were investigated. The different synthesis processes endow the distinct fluorescence properties and differentiated surface state of B-CDs, C-CDs, Y-CDs, and R-CDs, resulting the different fluorescence response to MNZ. Analysis of the fluorescence intensities of the multicolor CDs in the presence of MNZ demonstrated the different fluorescence response patterns. As shown in Fig. S6, B-CDs showed the decline in the fluorescence intensity when MNZ were introduced while the fluorescence intensity of Y-CDs was nearly unchanged. In contrast, C-CDs also showed the obvious decline in the fluorescence intensity while R-CDs showed the enhancement of fluorescence intensities (Fig. 3 ), and the change of fluorescence intensity was accompanied by the differentiated fluorescence color changes in the presence of different MNZ concentrations. In addition, the F/F0 of C-CDs or R-CDs display linear relationship with increasing concentrations of MNZ and the coefficient of determination are 0.9991 and 0.9837, respectively. The results demonstrate that the different fluorescence response provide a reliable strategy for ratiometric fluorescence detection of MNZ. Therefore, in this work, C-CDs and R-CDs were chosen to construct the fluorometric analysis platform for rapid quantification of MNZ and were also used as sensor elements to develop the fluorescence sensor array for discrimination of different NIIMs.

Moreover, the mechanism of the fluorescence response of C-CDs and R-CDs to MNZ was investigated. According to our previous study, the MNZ is negative in aqueous solution [35]. As shown in Fig. S5a, the Zeta potential of C-CDs and R-CDs is −5.23 mV and +5.15 mV, respectively. Therefore, there was no direct interaction between C-CDs and MNZ because of the electrostatic repulsion. The TEM image also suggested that the C-CDs were dispersed and no agglomeration was observed after the addition of MNZ (Fig. S7a). However, spectral data show that the absorption spectrum of MNZ is overlapped with the excitation wavelength of C-CDs (Fig. S7b), which is the condition of inner filter effect (IFE). Thus, the results indicated that the quenching of C-CDs is due to the IFE between C-CDs and MNZ. In contrast, R-CDs is positive (Fig. S5a), which resulted in the adsorption of MNZ on the R-CDs due to the electrostatic attraction. As shown in Fig. S7c, the nanoparticles were also dispersed after the addition of MNZ, suggesting that the adsorption of MNZ on the surface of R-CDs did not cause the aggregation of R-CDs and that the fluorescence variation was not stemming from the particle state variation. Generally, the enhancement of fluorescence is due to the passivation of the surface defect on the CDs [43,44]. To confirm the fact, the UV absorption spectra of R-CDs with different MNZ concentrations were obtained and showed that the absorbance of R-CDs at 600 nm increased with increasing MNZ concentrations (Fig. S7d). According to the literature, the enhancement of absorbance might due to the passivation of the surface defects on the CDs [45]. Thus, we speculate that the passivation of the surface defects on the R-CDs by MNZ give rise to the enhancement of fluorescence of R-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorometric quantification of MNZ based on an integrated analytical platform
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the different fluorescence response, the fluorometric analysis was developed for quantification of MNZ according to the change of fluorescence based on C-CDs and R-CDs. First, the C-CD + R-CD system was prepared and the fluorescence color of the C-CD + R-CD system with different volume ratios in the presence of MNZ was investigated. Obviously, the marked change of the fluorescence at the volume ratios of 5:7 was observed and the system with the volume ratio was used for further experiments (Fig. S8a). Then, the properties were studied and the response conditions toward MNZ were optimized. The results showed that the R/G values remain largely the same for a long time (6 days), indicating that the system was very stable (Fig. S8b). The eﬀect of pH on the ﬂuorescence changes was also investigated and the results showed that the intensity of C-CD + R-CD system remained virtually unchanged between pH 5 to 9 (Fig. S8c), indicating that the system was stable in a wide pH range. In this case, we set the pH to 7.0 to carry out the further experiments. As we all known that fast response time is an important parameter in food safety analysis and is highly desirable for on-site analysis. Then, the response time of the C-CD + R-CD system for MNZ were studied and the results suggested R/G values reached the maximum value within 5 min (Fig. S8d).

The above mentioned properties of the C-CD + R-CD system are beneficial for fluorometric quantification of MNZ. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the obvious fluorescence variations of the probe under UV lamp (365 nm) were obtained in the presence of MNZ. The fluorescence images were taken and the red-green-blue (RGB) values were collected by a smartphone. The linear fitting of R/G values against MNZ concentrations exhibited better linear correlation (R/G = 0.026X+1.01) with R2 = 0.994. The limit of detection (LOD) was 0.45 μM (LOD = 3σ/S, where σ is the standard deviation of the blank samples and S is the slope of the calibration plot) and limit of quantification (LOQ) was 1.5 μM (LOQ = 10σ/S). In addition, as shown in Fig. S9, the Studentized residual plots, response factor plots, and percent relative error of back-calculated concentrations (%RE) plots have also been obtained according the literature [46]. Although the response factor plot shows a high deviation, the Studentized residual plot shows all the computed residuals within the ±2 limits over the entire calibration range (Fig. S9a) and the %RE of most of back-calculated concentrations are distributed within the calculated acceptance limits (Fig. S9b). In view of these results, linearity can be assured in this calibration range according to the fitness for purpose approach. Therefore, without the fluorescence spectrometer, the rapid detection of MNZ with only UV lamp and a smartphone can be achieved. In addition, the selectivity of the C-CD + R-CD system was investigated. As the milk samples contain very high concentrations of KCl, the concentrations of K+ and Cl− ions (200 μM) were high compared with other ions and interferences (100 μM). As shown in Fig. 4b, only MNZ caused obvious enhancement of R/G compared with interferences, suggesting that our proposed method was high selective for MNZ.

In order to further simplify the analysis process, the integrated analytical platform was constructed. As shown in Fig. 4 c and d, the main parts of the platform included a laser lamp, a rotatable cuvette tank, and smartphone holder. The complete and detailed assembly process is shown in Fig. S1 and sizes of each part are shown in Fig. S10. The fluorescence images of samples under UV lamp can be obtained by smartphone and then are converted to RGB values based on an App in the smartphone. Finally, the rapid quantification of MNZ is achieved by converting the R/G values to the MNZ concentrations according to the corresponding calibration curves. Moreover, we have compared our results with that of the recent literature about the detection of MNZ (Table S1). Even though our established method is not all superior to these methods in previously published articles in terms of linear range, LOD, and recovery, the advantages of our method are obvious. For example, the preparation of the detection system is easy and repeatable, and the smartphone-integrated analysis platform is easy to operate without the use of complex and expensive equipment. In addition, the sensitivity can also meet the actual requirements completely. Moreover, the whole process of detection was within 20 min. Therefore, the simple structure makes the platform portable for rapid and on-site detection of MNZ, and the platform is expected to become a universal platform for quantitative analysis of other contamination of food.

In order to detection MNZ from the other NIIMs, fluorescence sensor array was designed for discriminating the different NIIMs. According to the above results, the C-CDs and R-CDs showed different fluorescence response to MNZ, and therefore they were used as sensor elements for simultaneously discrimination of different NIIMs. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the fluorescence response patterns were evaluated by the change in the fluorescence intensity (ΔF/F0, ΔF=F0–F, where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensity in the absence and presence of NIIMs, respectively). The C-CDs and R-CDs generated specific fingerprint patterns with five NIIMs based on the completely different fluorescence response, which confirmed the feasibility of the designed sensor array for identifying different NIIMs. LDA was used for the data analysis of the fluorescence sensor array and two discrimination factors were calculated. By the LDA analysis based on the two factors, different NIIMs were divided into diﬀerent clusters (Fig. 5b). In addition, radar maps (Fig. 5c) and heat maps (Fig. 5d), where the color and radius transitions correspond to the value of (F0–F)/F change from −0.6 to 0.4 at two channels, also showed that the five NIIMs can be easily distinguished by their diverse two-channel color or radius. The results suggested that the fluorescence sensor array has good discriminative ability.

The quantification of MNZ in food samples were carried out based on the fluorometric analysis to evaluate the application potential and feasibility. Two brands of honey (No.1 and No.2) and milk (No.1 and No.2) were used as food samples and a series of 0, 20, 40, and 60 μM MNZ standards were spiked. As shown in Table 1 , the recoveries of MNZ in food samples varied from 84.0 to 106.3 % and the relative standard deviations (RSD, n = 3) were in the range of 1.2–10.2 %. The spiked samples were also detected by high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) and the recoveries range from 91.3 to 100.3 %. The F test was performed and the results showed that there were no significant differences between our method and HPLC (Table S2), indicating that the proposed fluorometric method can be used in food samples. In addition, to confirm the sensitivity of the method in food samples, the recovery studies in lower concentrations (0.5, 1.5, and 5 μM) were also performed and the results showed the good recoveries (Table S3). The results demonstrated that the proposed method exhibited good accuracy, being free from matrix interference eﬀects. Moreover, the fluorometric analysis can be carried out in the smartphone-integrated platform, which increase the portability in on-site detection of MNZ in food samples.

In summary, we have developed a fluorometric analysis platform for determination and discrimination of NIIMs residues in food matrices based on multicolor CDs. The multicolor CDs were prepared by a simple hydrothermal method and they showed different fluorescence response to MNZ. The remarkable fluorescence quenching of C-CDs and an enhanced fluorescence of R-CDs resulted in the distinguished fluorescence color changes of C-CD + R-CD systems with increasing MNZ concentrations. Thus, fluorometric quantification of MNZ was achieved by transforming the fluorescence images into RGB values based on the smartphone-integrated platform. In addition, a fluorescence sensor array was constructed and has the good discrimination ability to discriminate five NIIMs. Moreover, the platform was employed for real food sample analysis and exhibited satisfactory performance for MNZ detection. Therefore, the proposed method provides new insight into the construction of rapid, on-site, and universal platform for drug residues detection, which is of great importance for food safety assessment.

Shaojie Wang: Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Yongbo Wang: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Project administration, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Yuanna Ning: Data curation, Methodology. Wencai Wang: Formal analysis, Investigation. Qiming Liu: Formal analysis, Methodology.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: We declare that no conflict of interest exits in the submission of this manuscript, and the manuscript is approved by all authors for publication. I would like to declare on behalf of my co-authors that the work described was original research that has not been published previously, and not under consideration for publication elsewhere, in whole or in part.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (32001783), the Science and Technology Program of Shaanxi Province (2019JQ-484), and the Scientific Research Program Funded by Shaanxi Provincial Education Department (20JK0540).

ce:section.ce:para:
